textauszug: BURNHAM & ROOT commerce & architecture JOHN WELLBORN ROOT „ ... but to apply the constructive principles of the best Gothic work of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries of the materials, facilities and necessities of our time... „ Peter Bonnet Wight zu John Root „I admire Root‘s stance at the drawing board, the strength of his arms, the whiteness of his skin, and his frank smile“ Daniel Burnham DANIEL HUDSON BURNHAM THE SHERMAN HOUSE 1874 „This house is posessing a certain allure or style indicating personality“ Louis Sullivan BURNHAM & ROOT commerce & architecture WOMAN‘S TEMPLE 1890-92 „It may be mentioned in passing, that the partnership between these two gentlemen seems to be admirably adjusted, Mr. Burnham adding to keen artistic perception remarkable executive ability.“ Architects of Chicago, 1890 von John Wellborn Root THE ROOKERY 1886 Vergleich mit Marshall Field's Wholesale Store von H.H. Richardson1887 JOHN WELLBORN ROOT THE MONADNOCK THE RELIANCE 1890 – 1895 „ ... but to apply the constructive principles of the best Gothic work of the twelfth and thirteenth centuries of the materials, facilities and necessities of our time... „ Peter Bonnet Wight zu John Root THE ROOKERY THE MONADNOCK THE RELIANCE „I admire Root‘s stance at the drawing board, the strength of his arms, the whiteness of his skin, and his frank smile“ Daniel Burnham DANIEL HUDSON BURNHAM THE SHERMAN HOUSE 1874 „This house is posessing a certain allure or style indicating personality“ Louis Sullivan GRANNIS BLOCK 1880-81 MONTAUK BUILDING 1881-82 MASONIC TEMPLE 1890-92 Burnham and Root Monadnock Building Chicago 1889 25 References 27 References Bibliography Books Donald Hoffmann, John Root’s Monadnock Building, in: Journal oft the Society of Ar - chitectural historians, Vol. 26, No. 4, 1967 . Internet http://www.monadnockbuilding.com/Building%20plans.htm http://memory.loc.gov/ammem/index.html http://chicagoinmaps.com/historicmaps.html http://www.lib.uchicago.edu/e/su/maps/chi1900/ 26 FS 2012 The Tall Office Building Artitistically Considered Section, 1:750 Plan Elevation 4.464.086.365.056.365.056.365.056.365.266.041.074.017.197.194.107.197.194.107.197.194.80 123.15 3.553.603.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.413.894.183.83 62.77 1 : 100 4.464.086.365.056.365.056.365.056.365.266.041.074.017.197.194.107.197.194.107.197.194.80 123.15 3.553.603.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.663.413.894.183.83 62.77 1 : 100 42.5 31.07.11 11:33print Seite 1 von 2http://www.cca.qc.ca/en/print_feature/320 Burnham and Root Six drawings for the Monadnock Building (Chicago, Ill.), between March 1885 and May 1889 Lead pencil on tracing paper and vellum 93.5 x 52.4 cm. and smaller DR1986:0767:005 The Monadnock Building, also known as the Monadnock Block, occupies a curious place in the architectural history of the United States. It is the tallest load-bearing masonry building in Chicago and a harbinger of the steel skeleton skyscraper. It is a refined example of commercial architecture from the Chicago School and, at the same time, an example of traditional building technique and classical decorative motives. The building is two buildings in one – designed by different architects, pointing to the future and the past. The north side of the building was designed by Burnham and Root (Daniel H. Burnham, 1846- 1912 and John Wellborn Root, 1850-1891) for the Boston developer Peter Brooks and built in 1889-1891. The design is notable for the lack of ornamentation on the exterior. The buildingʼs character comes instead from the clarity of its form and the expressiveness of the building materials. For example, the base of the building has walls that are six feet thick and flare out, an indication of the weight of the 15 floors above. The south side of the Monadnock Block was developed by Peter Brooksʼs brother, Shepard Brooks, and designed by Holabird & Roche (Chicago, Ill.). This part of the Monadnock was built in 1891-1893 and is an early example of steel frame construction. The steel allows the greater spans and larger openings that appear on this part of the building. While the north and south halves are similar in proportion and in their large details, the south building does not have the severe simplicity of the Burnham and Root design. The CCA Collection owns over 500 drawings, blueprints and other reprographic copies made for the design and construction of this iconic Chicago building. Atwood, Daniel Hudson Burnham Technische Daten Höhe bis zum Dach: 61 m Etagen:15 Nutzfläche:39.000 m! Baustoff:Aluminium Glas Stahl Terrakotta Fassade Reliance Building aus Wikipedia, der freien Enzyklopädie Das Reliance Building ist ein Hochhaus in der US- amerikanischen Stadt Chicago und war der erste Wolkenkratzer, dessen Fassade überwiegend mit Glas verkleidet war. Architektur Der Bau wurde in zwei Phasen errichtet. Das Erdgeschoss und der erste Stock wurden von John Wellborn Root entworfen. Die darüber liegenden Geschosse wurden von Charles B. Atwood ausgeführt[1], der dem 1891 verstorbenen „John Root“ im Architekturbüro von Daniel Hudson Burnham folgte. Der Wechsel ist in der Architektur zu sehen. Das Untergeschoss ist ohne Ornamente und dunkel verkleidet, während die Fassade der 13 Bürogeschosse offen und transparent gestaltet sind. Atwood setzt das sogenannte Chicago-Fenster ein, bei dem eine große Glasscheibe vor die Fassadenebene gestellt ist, während zwei schmale Scheiben an den Seiten geöffnet werden können.
